title: JSON — The Universal Data Format
subtitle: How computers share structured information

sections.content:
When a server sends data back to your app, it needs a format both sides understand. The most popular format on the web is **JSON** (JavaScript Object Notation).

JSON is a way to write structured data as plain text. It looks a lot like a simple list or form — it has names (called **keys**) paired with values, and it can contain lists and nested groups of data.

Even though it has "JavaScript" in the name, JSON is used by every programming language. It's the universal data format of the web.
sections.analogy: JSON is like a well-organized form. A form has labeled fields ("Name: ___", "Email: ___") and you fill in the values. JSON works the same way: each piece of data has a label (key) and a value. And just like a form can have sections ("Address: Street ___, City ___, Zip ___"), JSON can have nested groups.
keyPoints: JSON = JavaScript Object Notation — a plain-text data format
Uses curly braces `{}` for objects (groups of key-value pairs)
Uses square brackets `[]` for arrays (ordered lists)
Objects can contain other objects — this is called **nesting**

sections.instruction: Here's what JSON looks like in practice. This is the kind of data a server might send when you view someone's profile.
sections.explanation: Notice the structure: "name" is a simple text value, "age" is a number, "hobbies" is an array (a list in square brackets), and "address" is a nested object (another group of key-value pairs inside curly braces). Everything has a clear label, making it easy for any program to read.

sections.instruction: Match each JSON concept with its description.

sections.question: In JSON, what does a pair of square brackets [ ] represent?
sections.explanation: Square brackets in JSON create an array — an ordered list of values. For example, ["reading", "hiking", "photography"] is an array of three strings. Curly braces {} create objects.

completionMessage: You now understand JSON, the universal data format of the web. You've completed Level 5 and have a solid understanding of how software and the web work: code, languages, websites, clients, servers, HTTP, APIs, and JSON. These are the building blocks everything else is built on.
